text: ORNL WELDING PROGRAMMER-CONTROLLER, CONTROLS AND FUNCTIONS 41

section: 10.Commercial U.S.Equipment Sources For Orbital Welding Systems 39
text: RECOMMENDED FORMAT FOR ESTABLISHING A WELDING PROCEDURE FOR AUTOMATIC WELDING 45
In 1968 ORNL reviewed the methods and equipment used to make remote repairs in the high-radiation zones of several nuclear reactor systems.
Our work produced operational automated pipe welding systems for butt welding pipes from 3 to 16 in. in diameter.
However, we encountered difficulties in trying to apply the system carriage schemes to pipes larger than 16 in. and were directed by the AEC to terminate work with large carriages.
With commercial equipment, we performed successful tests on a 28-in. pipe weld.
This interest, however, seemed to develop most rapidly after our Industrial Cooperation Conference on Automated Pipe Welding in February 1971.
More than 100 representatives of industry and utilities attended the conference, and since that time, several companies have seriously entered the automated pipe welding field.
At present, there are different orbiting carriages for the following ranges of pipe (outside) diameters: 3 to 6, 6 to 9, 9 to 12, and 12 to 16 in.
In these carriages, starting with the 6- to 9-in. carriage, the subassemblies and parts are interchangeable, as are the welding heads; this helps to minimize the spare parts inventory.
Figure 1 illustrates a typical automated
2 shows an in-process weld on a 12-in. pipe.
A Lincoln TIG 300/300 power supply was used in our system.
We use a Gulton model TR-888 recorder.
Figures 3 and 4 show the carriage, its components, and subassemblies.

carriage is $4^{1/2}$ in. on the pipe radius and $10^{3/4}$ in. longitudinally. [Note: The $4^{1/2}$ -in. radial clearance is adequate only for cutting and for welding without automatic arc length controls (electrode-to-work gap controls).
A carriage/weld-head combination with automatic arc length controls requires $5^{3/4}$ in. radial clearance.]

The carriage is made to rotate by a direct drive system from motors mounted at the two hinge points of the actuating arms within the rollers. [Note: An exception is the 3- to 6-in. carriage, where the motors are not concentric with the rollers]
A standard 4-in.-diam., $2\frac{1}{2}$ -lb wire spool is used to store enough wire for multiple weld filler passes.
Figure 5 is a top view of the head with the lid in the closed (welding) position, showing the power and control cable entry to the head, and Fig.
6 is a side elevation view showing the hinged lid in the open position.

The oscillator assembly consists of a 24-V dc drive motor, an adjustable cam, and a bearing-supported crank arm that attaches to and drives (rocks) the torch block electrode holder assembly via a tapered pin.
The cam is adjustable for total electrode tip oscillation from zero to $5/8$ in.
The oscillator motor speed can be adjusted to provide oscillation frequencies of 50 to $360~\mathrm{cpm}$ , as discussed in detail in Section 4.

The wire feed system consists of a wire spool containing a preselected type and size of wire, the guide tube, the feed mechanism with its 24-V tachometer-gear-head motor, the ferrule, and the exit nozzle.

The wire is prevented from moving toward the center of the rotor disk by a shoulder, and movement of the wire toward the periphery of the disks is prevented by the outer disk face being canted at $8^{\circ}$ to the rotor disk face.
The mechanism is designed to accommodate wire diameters from $\frac{1}{32}$ to $\frac{1}{16}$ in. and feed rates up to 50 in./min.
We use a Lincoln model TIG 300/300 power supply, Fig.
13, with minor internal circuit modifications.
14, have been added to provide for programmed operation control of the current in the welder's saturable reactor.
The controls are normally set for $12\mathrm{ft}^3$ of gas per hour and $\frac{1}{3}$ gpm of water, but can be set at other values if desired.
15 is a lightweight, portable, remote-operation station designed to be held in the weld operator's hand.
16 is a current transformer-thyrector combination housed in an anodized aluminum box.
The transformer features a toroidal coil encased in plastic, with a 1-in.-diam center opening passage for the weld cable.

Programmer lines include a 120-V (5-A) ac power supply, a signal control cable to the weld power supply, a current sensor line, a remote-control pendant cable, a signal cable to the recorder filter box and recorder, and a control cable to the weld head.
The weld power source includes a 460-V (60-A) ac supply as well as water and gas supplies.
The stepper relay has 51 active positions and a home position.
Positions 1 to 5 are used for prepurge, 6 to 10 for upslope, 11 to 30 for weld, 31 to 39 for downslope, and 40 to 51 for postpurge.
There are 8 banks of contacts on the stepper relay.
Banks 1 and 2 determine the positions at which the prepurge, upslope, weld, and downslope and postpurge operations occur.
Stepper banks 3 and 4 determine the position at which the carriage starts and at which the wire feed starts and stops.

Stepper banks 5 and 6, together with a Vernistat mounted on the front panel, provide a means of setting the desired current profile in 34 discrete steps during the upslope, weld, and downslope operations.
The Vernistat is composed of 34 sliders which are sequentially selected as the stepper advances through positions 6 to 39 respectively.
The reference voltage represents 0 to $100\%$ of "maximum welding current." An additional digital dial is provided on the front panel to set the maximum welding current to any value up to 200 A.
Stepper banks 5 and 6 are used to operate lights to indicate the position of the stepper switch throughout the program.
This unit modulates the welding current servo amplifier at either 40 or $60~\mathrm{cpm}$ and is switch selectable from the front panel.
Pulse amplitude is dialed directly by a potentiometer on the front panel that is calibrated 0 to $100\mathrm{A}$ peak to peak.
An illuminated potentiometer dial on the front panel calibrated 50 to $360~\mathrm{cpm}$ furnishes the signal to the Darlington pair.
When the AVC wire switch is off, the output of the AVC wire module is clamped at $+30\mathrm{V}$ dc and the wire feed rate is insensitive to variations in arc voltage.
2. $+30\mathrm{V}$ dc regulated,
3. $\pm 15\mathrm{V}$ dc regulated (ALC).
4. $\pm 15\mathrm{V}$ dc regulated (pulser),
5. $+40\mathrm{V}$ dc (nominal) unregulated.
The $+40\mathrm{V}$ dc is supplied from the welder power supply when the welder contactor is closed, that is, during the welding cycle.

section: 5. Welding Studies, Weld Joint Development, and Welding Schedule
text: ORNL purposely omitted experimentation with consumable EB (Electric Boat Company, Groton, Conn.) type inserts, because EB insert welding requires pipe inside diameter matchup within 5-mil limits, which is too expensive to prepare for field construction pipe welding.
Joint mismatch, one cause for poor root pass welds, especially in the pipe horizontal position, is most critical in the "overhead," or 6 o'clock location.
Helium requires only about $60\%$ of the welding current required for an equivalent weld with argon shield gas due to the increased arc voltage.
The pipe interiors were purged with argon usually at approximately 20 cfh gas flow.
Electrode configuration tests have indicated that better welds can be obtained by tapering the electrode to a $30^{\circ}$ included angle and providing a minimum rounded-off apex.
The driver and idler rollers are designed to include two $\frac{1}{2}$ -in.-wide metal lands, one at each end of the 6-in.-wide Viton rubber roll tread surface.

The lands project to within 0.005 in. of the rubber surface, so that when the rollers are clamped to the pipe, the rubber is deformed at the point of contact to the extent that the metal lands contact the pipe surface.
The traction obtainable from these rollers drives the carriage around the horizontal pipe with a maximum deviation of about 3 to 6 mils per orbit.
No compensation is required for this amount of drift, and no end restraints are required for welding pipes from horizontal to $15^{\circ}$ slope positions.
However, when welding on pipes that slope more than $15^{\circ}$ end restraints are required in order to keep the rollers from slipping.

section: GUIDELINES FOR TACKING CONSUMABLE WELD INSERTS
text: The insert is supported by the clamp and held in position over the pipe end; the tack welds on one side of a joint should be about 1 in. apart.

section: GUIDELINES FOR TACKING CONSUMABLE WELD INSERTS
text: When tacking an insert to both sides of a pipe joint, the tack welds should be alternated to the respective pipe ends, with at least six approximately equally spaced tack welds around each pipe, or so that they are no more than $3/4$ in. apart, whichever distance is smaller.

section: GUIDELINES FOR TACKING CONSUMABLE WELD INSERTS
text: For gas tungsten-arc tack welding, the arc current should be set at approximately $75\%$ of the mean root pass weld current, or about 80 A at 12 V.
Before welding, a minimum of 6 in. of the internal surface of the joint on each side of the weld should be blanketed with argon.

The volume of gas for the blanket should be at least five times the volume of the area to be blanketed, and the blanket should be maintained during welding and until the temperature of the weld falls below $400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Table 1 is a typical data sheet for our system giving the programmer input information for the root and subsequent filler pass welds.
Gas flow should be 14 cfh minimum and water flow should be about $\frac{1}{2}$ gpm at approximately 20 psi.

section: GUIDELINES FOR THE WELDER-OPERATOR'S VISUAL OBSERVATION DURING AUTOMATED WELDING
text: A near 12 o'clock start position for the first fill pass (for 5G position welding) generally helps to balance partial distortion of the pipe created by the root pass.

section: GUIDELINES FOR THE WELDER-OPERATOR'S VISUAL OBSERVATION DURING AUTOMATED WELDING
text: One procedure for filling local weld areas that have been purposely ground out to remove weld defects is to place the carriage about $\frac{1}{2}$ in. in front of the area to be filled, set a short arc gap of about $\frac{1}{3}$ in., and initiate a weld cycle.

section: Recommended Format for Establishing a Welding Procedure for Automatic Welding
text: A sample welding procedure is given along with a typical list of general welding parameters (Table B-1).

section: Recommended Format for Establishing a Welding Procedure for Automatic Welding
text: Figures B-1 and B-2 illustrate a joint geometry for weld joints and details of insert tack-weld sequencing respectively.
ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section IX, "Welding Qualifications," and RDT Standards E 15-2T and F 6-5T, "Requirements for Nuclear Components" and "Welding Qualifications"
Specification - ASTM A 312-64, "Specification for Seamless and Welded Austenitic Stainless Steel Pipe."
Diameter - 6.625-in. nominal outside.
F and A numbers per Code Section IX - type ER F7 (Table Q11.2), Weld Metal Analysis No. = A7 (Table Q11.3).
Form - continuous length wound on 4-in. spools.
Welding-grade argon at 20-cfh flow rate during welding; prepurge prior to welding as described in paragraph 2(a)iii.
B-2, mating ends of the pipe are to be tack welded to each other, with an appropriately placed insert.
Electrode $-3/32$ -in.-diam nominal $2\%$ thoriated tungsten, AWS classification EWTh-2, to AWS Specification A 5.12-69, "Specification for Tungsten-Arc Welding Electrodes."
Flowmeter - suitable for controlling the flow of argon within $\pm 1$ cfh, actual setting for pressure, 50 psig.
Before welding starts, a minimum of 3 in. of the internal surface of the joint on each side of the weld should be blanketed with argon.

The back side of the joint should be purged with a minimum of $20\mathrm{cfh}$ of argon for at least $10\mathrm{min}$ prior to root-pass welding and for at least 5 min prior to the first two fill passes.
This area is to remain blanketed during welding, and until the temperature of the weld falls below $250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
